speaker: interviewee
text: I mentioned that there was this uprising. And that ultimately, we somehow got back into our camp, after maybe a delay of three, four hours. And then I was asked how those gas chambers were destroyed. From the bits and pieces, from what I know and heard, in camp two, just as I related in camp one, were the trustees, like the people that worked in the German offices, keeping record. I'm talking about the penal camp. They had trustees in camp two, as well. And somehow they made their way into the ammunition chambers with the guns and the ammunition. From what I understand, they had-- got a number of hand grenades and [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] the type of material that sets buildings on fire in no time, incendiary devises, I believe they're called. And they made their way out and did away and killed quite a few of the guards, and the Nazis, and the Germans. And they threw those hand grenades into the gas chambers and the-- the-- the ovens and-- and all the different areas. They even set a fire piles of clothing and hair and stuff like that as they escaped.

speaker: interviewer
text: The other thing I was asked, who were the guards? Well, the guards were basically [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] SS. They had the-- the-- the camp leaders, the commandants, were-- were colonels, rank of colonels, or captains, or higher that ran the camps. And the key individuals that ran the camps were the SS people, German SS. But most of them in both camps were Ukrainians and Lithuanians that worked for the Nazis.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- the-- the Ukrainians, I suppose it's known just about to most people, these are the Ukrainians that were part of the Russian army in 1941 when a division of 40,000 Russian soldiers, under the direction of General [? Vlasov, ?] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] ran away from the Russians and went over to the Nazis. They gave themselves up because Ukraine, as we even know more so today, they weren't so in love with the Russians themselves. So there were a lot of ethnic Germans in the Ukraine. And they-- these were most of the guards in many of the camps throughout Poland and other areas, in particular. So now there was-- we have the uprising. We got back into the-- into our camp, Treblinka I. We learned what was going on. Again, they lined us up. Many of the prisoners and workers that were left behind, most of them, were done away with. The group that came back from [INAUDIBLE] or [INAUDIBLE] respectively, was a new selection, because they were trimming the [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] prisoners to absolute limit, limited group, to 550.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the morning, there were 3,000 some odd. Before the day was over, part of the ones that were done away with before we returned from work, and additional ones that came from the work detail that spent the day in-- working on the railroads. Was again a selection. And again, fortunately for me, myself and another young gentleman, about-- a young man about my age, by the name of David Taup from Berlin, we were selected to be water carriers, which was a very important post.

speaker: interviewee
text: The water carriers before, they were involved in the uprising, made duplicate keys to the gun chambers, and so on. And they were done away with. So once again, this tall, elderly SS man, by the name of Untersturmfuhrer Pravey from Kaiserslautern in Germany, which we knew, selected to stay with Taup and me to become water carriers, which probably was the best job in the entire camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did he select you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The water carrier-- I don't know why he selected me. Maybe I looked young. Maybe I looked fit. Maybe he could sense because I spoke German he could converse with me. Maybe I looked-- I-- I had a little life in me, and that's what he really needed. The water carriers, there was very little. Treblinka is in an elevated area, maybe a few hundred feet above sea level. And there wasn't all that much water, so naturally, you had to pump the water and dug deep ditches and made--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wells.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wells, where the water had to be cranked up with a pail, and then had to be carried over to the guard's kitchen, and to the officer's kitchen. So the water was carried-- was pumped in the inner camp, but had to be carried out into the outer camp, as you will see on this map. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the cook was Ukrainian, one was [INAUDIBLE], and the other one was [INAUDIBLE]. He liked us. And we always put some leftovers under the pail as we went back.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there were 550 that somehow, because no new transports came. So for the next 11 and a half months, tranquility existed. So who were the 550 people? The professionals, the carpenters, the blacksmiths, the tinsmiths, the tailors, the cooks, the electricians, whoever could perform some work. The group that had got used to work the water carriers, like myself. Why did they need so many?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their-- the tailors made uniforms for them and for other people. The carpenters made furnitures for them to send home to Germany. The blacksmiths they'd create-- and silversmiths, all kinds of things, they made jewelry, all for them. They could use them. And so there were couples with children, and some couples without children. In fact, they had separate barracks. They lived together. Not necessarily were they married, but even before I came, some of those, the Nazis said, OK you his wife. You his wife. You, you're her husband. And that-- they made up the couples. And that's the way it was. And there was one by the name of Rikno. He was the chief of the Jewish man. He came from the ghetto, when they liquidated the ghetto of [? Otwock. ?] You have heard of [? Otwock? ?] Near Warsaw. So they saw it before the war. And that Rikno, he was a-- an engineer, an electrical engineer. He built the-- the-- the turbine that provided the-- the electricity for the camp. Mention him now because he comes into play later on. And everything was just basically wonderful. We had fairly good food. The beatings and the killings were dramatically reduced. Once in a while, they caught some people that wanted to escape, so they-- we had to line up and they hung them right in front of us. But that was-- as bad as it, it was somehow bearable in relation to what we experienced before the uprising. And I sort of feel that they spared us because they needed us. And no new transports came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Treblinka II didn't have any gas chambers. They didn't rebuild it. Now it's already 19-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] beginning fall of 1943. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And less than one year later the Russians, the-- the-- and the war effort for the Germans already was in decline. Things went in the opposite direction for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew that. We sensed it. Later on, I'll get into it a little bit more how we knew. And we just sensed, no more, they needed us. But my job in particular, as a water carrier, it was superb. But once in a while, you know, yes, we made our runs from the inner camp to the outer and back, we encountered some of the die hard SS. And they had a chance-- they had to kick us or beat us. They-- but we ran fast, and it was insignificant in relation to when I worked in [? Melchena ?] and [INAUDIBLE] because the beatings were terrible. And the bodies-- I somehow was able to escape many of those. But I had my share, too. So now life stabilized. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But then in early [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] July 1944, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we just heard that the war efforts were going not so well for the Nazis. Then we heard the big guns from the distance. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as we heard those big guns, the Russian front was coming closer. We knew, no way under no circumstances would the Nazis allow any of us to survive to tell the world what we experienced and what we saw. And we saw a lot. We-- over the year and one week that I was in Treblinka, not to mention the previous four-- four years, but in particular in Treblinka, have seen hundreds, and hundreds, and hundreds of people being shot to death, beaten to death, tortured to death, hung, beaten to death with a trunk of wood, for a variety of reasons, or no reasons. So we knew our days would be numbered. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] On July [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] 14, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just exactly one week before they eliminated the camp-- was the 14th or the 16th-- 5 days before, it must have been-- would have been the 16th. 16th, that's correct. Five days-- 16th and 17th. I have it recorded. 1944. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were preparing to escape. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we prepared ourselves. We had all our orders. There was only one barrack that was divided in the group that had families, and wives, husbands, respectively. Some of them were true marriages. Some of them were arranged marriages. And the other side for the men, most of them were men, which was the-- the workers' barrack, we knew what we were going to do. We were going to, about 5 o'clock the next morning, to escape the camp. And all 550 would run towards the fences. There are three types, the inner fence, the middle fence, and then the-- the-- the-- the [INAUDIBLE] nets, which is the-- the kind of wire where you can easily get caught in it. That we would take our blankets, we would storm the fences, we would do away with the guards. We figured maybe 70% to 80% of us would die, but some of us would survive, no chance, because of a mass uprising no way that someone could not survive. We had our orders towards a careful plan. We went to bed. Some of us were involved to a limited extent, like myself. I didn't know that much about-- they just told me what to do. There was a higher up group of the prisoners that was-- were more intelligent. They know what to do, and so on. They gave us their orders.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to our bunks and laid down. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] About 11:00, 12:00 in the evening, the Nazis come in, one, this Untersturmfuhrer Lantz, who I testified in Vienna in the war crimes trials. I testified in five, by the way. That I subsequently in 1962, I was at his trial in Vienna. He came in, looked around, didn't ask-- check if people are dressed or not. And left.

speaker: interviewee
text: When he left, a few minutes later, we were told we have to forget this uprising, because we understand they're aware when our plans was. And this electrical engineer that built-- Rikno-- that built that locomobile, which creates the-- the turbine for the generator, for the light generators, he was a trustee of the Nazis. He had a wife and two children there. He was the [INAUDIBLE]. He told them. And that annihilated-- that-- that did away with our plans. He went to the Nazis. That's a known fact.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we didn't. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So life went on. The guns came closer. On the 23rd of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] July, 1944, it was a Sunday. There was a dispute whether it was the 23rd or was the 25th, or what it was. I settled by going to the library, and I showed you before, that one piece-- we knew it was a Sunday, what-- which was [INAUDIBLE]. It was the 23rd, because I wanted to know what happened on that day in the world. I believe you have it there. That other piece of paper that you have. Yes, we'll take a picture of it later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In any case, on that morning, it was a Sunday, I would say about 11 o'clock. I was-- we did, by the way, we-- you asked before would our working be, especially that last 11 months or so, it was a six day a week working arrangement. And Sundays we didn't work to care for ourselves, rest and that. But we were fairly well treated, fairly well. That doesn't mean good, only if you compared how you were treated before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the food better also?

speaker: interviewee
text: The food was better. Not a lot better, but somewhat better. More plentiful, more bread. There was a bakery for the--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the food, by the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: The food was bread and soup, sometimes a little horse meat, maybe a little bit potato. If you're lucky, you got more of the heavy stuff. If you're not lucky you got mostly the water. It all depends, even inside the camp, the-- the guy that gave the soup was a prisoner, too. If he liked you, you-- you get more from the bottom. If he don't like you, you get more from the top, just the soup. I was near the latrine. And we noticed a lot of activity. You could look through the barbed wires, a lot of activity, a lot of cars, a lot of trucks. They had brought in reinforcements, soldiers, more Ukrainians, more SS, and more guards surrounded the camp. And about 11:30 or so they stormed the inner camp with their of guns in their hands and came through the main gate in the inner camp and say, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Lay down wherever you are. And people laid down. Some magic power [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] gave me a signal. And I was delirious. And I felt that's the end. But my subconscious mind led me back into my barrack, jumped out the back window, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ran across the back yard in that area, 20, 30 yards, to a shed where there was a lot of lumber stored. I crawled between the lumber, put some boards over myself, and sat there. Then I heard, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Hurry up. The Ukrainians is hollered from the tower. Hurry up, one was hiding. Fraction of a minute later, a number of people came, guards. I could see them. They couldn't see me. And couldn't-- and looked around and put more boards on top of me. They said to themselves, we have no time for that. And we find him later on. And I was sitting there for hours. And I heard-- and later on I was told how it happened. They put groups of four and six. The men had to drop their pants so they couldn't run, just could walk. And it's 500 yards to the edge of the forest, which graves we dug ourselves over the months and weeks before, supposedly. Of course, those graves were-- already had hundreds, and hundreds, and hundreds of people in over the period when the camps were created. And they put them-- they had to walk towards the edge of the grave, and then shot them. I [? bet you've ?] seen many videos, because it's basically how they do it. I heard it-- I heard the shooting all day and all night. Then it got quiet. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] About-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and by the way, the shed that I was in was covered. Then it got dark. And then I heard airplanes. Then I heard the rain fall. It was raining. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the lights start surrounded the camp were turned off. And then there was-- then the Allied planes-- I don't know if they were Russians or whether from the Western forces-- they dropped a parachute flare that illuminated the entire area like it's daytime. Only one flare, and I could see it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] About half an hour it would drop and that was the end. It got very quiet. Then afterwards, I started to get-- I thought I'd make my way out. And as I tried, some of the lumbar shifted. Seconds later, two guards looked and came. Couldn't find me. I crawled back. It was dark. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I said to myself, now-- I said to myself, as I was sitting there, and I heard many, many others make the same-- many survivors, make the same statement. I said to myself, I believe I'm the only Jew in all Europe alive, I said to myself. And I heard that comment many times before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Obviously, it wasn't so. But I'm alive. But I'm inside the camp. I said, how am I going to get out of this camp? After all, you've got one barbed wire section, then there's two yards in between. There's another barbed wire section. Then about 300 feet further out, you have an anti-tank with those steel horses still, over which [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] nets, they used to call it, which is a net, metal net, when you put your foot in there, or anything, you get caught. You can't get out of it again. How am I going to get out of that?
